back_translation: Joash was seven years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem forty years. His mother’s name was Zibiah, from Beersheba.
Joash did what was right in the eyes of the LORD all the days of Jehoiada the priest.
Jehoiada found two wives for him, and he had sons and daughters.
Some time later, Joash set his heart on repairing the house of the LORD.

He summoned the priests and Levites and said, “Go out to the cities of Judah and collect money from all Israel year by year to repair the house of your God. Do it quickly.” But the Levites did not hurry.

So the king summoned Jehoiada the high priest and said, “Why have you not required the Levites to bring from Judah and Jerusalem the tax that Moses the servant of the LORD and the assembly of Israel appointed for the Tent of the Testimony?”
For the sons of Athaliah, that wicked woman, had broken into the house of God, and had even used the holy things of the house of the LORD for the Baals.
At the king’s command, they made a chest and set it outside at the gate of the house of the LORD.
Then a proclamation was made throughout Judah and Jerusalem to bring to the LORD the tax that Moses the servant of God had appointed for Israel in the wilderness.
All the officials and all the people rejoiced and brought their offerings and dropped them into the chest until it was full.

Whenever the Levites brought the chest to the king’s officers and they saw that there was much money, the royal scribe and the officer of the high priest would come and empty the chest, then carry it back to its place. They did this every day and collected much money.

The king and Jehoiada gave the money to those who supervised the work on the house of the LORD. They hired stonecutters and carpenters to restore the house of the LORD, and also workers in iron and bronze to repair the house of the LORD.
So the workmen labored, and the work of repair progressed in their hands. They restored the house of God to its former condition according to the plan, and strengthened it.

When they had finished, they brought the rest of the money to the king and Jehoiada, and it was made into vessels for the house of the LORD—vessels for the service and for the burnt offerings, dishes, and vessels of gold and silver. They offered burnt offerings in the house of the LORD regularly all the days of Jehoiada.
Jehoiada grew old and full of days, and he died; at his death he was 130 years old.
They buried him in the City of David with the kings, because he had done good in Israel, both toward God and toward His house.
After Jehoiada died, the officials of Judah came and paid homage to the king, and the king listened to them.
They abandoned the house of the LORD, the God of their fathers, and served the Asherah poles and idols. So wrath came upon Judah and Jerusalem for this guilt of theirs.
Yet He sent prophets to them to bring them back to the LORD; the prophets warned them, but they would not listen.

Then the Spirit of God came upon Zechariah son of Jehoiada the priest. He stood before the people and said to them, “Thus says God: Why do you transgress the commandments of the LORD so that you cannot prosper? Because you have forsaken the LORD, He has also forsaken you.”
But they conspired against him, and at the king’s command they stoned him to death in the courtyard of the house of the LORD.

So King Joash did not remember the kindness that Jehoiada, Zechariah’s father, had shown him, but instead executed Jehoiada’s son. As Zechariah was dying, he said, “May the LORD see and call you to account.”
At the end of the year, the army of Aram came up against Joash. They entered Judah and Jerusalem and destroyed all the leaders of the people, and sent all the plunder to the king at Damascus.

Although the army of Aram came with only a few men, the LORD delivered a very great army into their hand, because Judah had forsaken the LORD, the God of their fathers. So judgment was executed on Joash.

When the Arameans had withdrawn from him (they left him severely wounded), his servants conspired against him because of the blood of the son of Jehoiada the priest, and they killed him on his bed. So he died, and they buried him in the City of David, but not in the tombs of the kings.
These are those who conspired against him: Zabad son of Shimeath the Ammonitess, and Jehozabad son of Shimrith the Moabitess.
As for his sons, the many prophecies about him, and the restoration of the house of God, they are written in the treatise of the Book of the Kings. And Amaziah his son reigned in his place.
